aids.title: Matrix vs List: Spreadsheet vs Contacts
aids.content: Adjacency matrix is like a full classroom attendance grid (every student-student pair has a cell). Adjacency list is like a phone contacts list (each person only lists people they actually know). For a small class, the grid is fine. For a city of millions, only contacts make sense.
aids.forRemembering: When to choose adjacency matrix vs adjacency list

aids.title: Space Complexity: M-V-squared, L-V-plus-E
aids.content: Matrix = V-Squared (M and S both have sharp angles). List = V+E (L looks like a plus sign turned). Matrix: O(V^2). List: O(V+E).
aids.forRemembering: Space complexities of the two graph representations

aids.title: Symmetric Matrix Shortcut
aids.content: For undirected graphs, the adjacency matrix is symmetric. In exams, you only need to fill the upper triangle and mirror it. This also means you can store only n(n-1)/2 entries to save space.
aids.forRemembering: Adjacency matrix symmetry property for undirected graphs

aids.title: Count List Nodes Trick
aids.content: For undirected: total list nodes = 2 * edges (each edge stored twice). For directed: total list nodes = edges (each edge stored once). Use this to verify your adjacency list construction in exams.
aids.forRemembering: Verifying adjacency list correctness by counting total nodes
